FINAL ORDER APPROVING SETTLEMENT - On August 29, 2018, this court held a conference to evaluate the fairness of the settlement agreement reached by the parties in this Fair Labor Standards Act and employment-discrimination lawsuit. See Lynns Food Stores, Inc. v. United States, 679 F.2d 1350, 1353 (11th Cir. 1982) (stating that district courts must determine whether settlement agreements in FLSA lawsuits are fair and reasonable). The court, having considered the terms of the parties settlement and having questioned counsel about the basis of the settlement during the conference, APPROVES the parties settlement as fair and reasonable. The court DISMISSES this case WITH PREJUDICE, costs taxed as paid. The court RETAINS jurisdiction solely to enforce the settlement agreement. Signed by Chief Judge Karon O Bowdre on 9/5/2018. (KEK) (Entered: 09/05/2018)

SCHEDULING ORDER. If the parties are unable to resolve the dispute informally, they shall jointly contact chambers at (202) 354-3400 to schedule a teleconference. Thereafter, the parties shall prepare a joint email to send to the Court at Friedrich_Chambers@dcd.uscourts.gov that provides a clear, concise description of the issues in dispute. Judge George A. O'Toole, Jr: ELECTRONIC ORDER entered. Peter A. Hern has filed a suggestion of bankruptcy, triggering an automatic stay against him pursuant to 11 U.S.C. \xc2\xa7 362(a). The action is stayed against Hern pending further order of the Court. Additionally, the Court has an obligation to inquire sua sponte into its own subject matter jurisdiction. The jurisdictional facts advanced by the plaintiff in its complaint are insufficient to establish complete diversity in this action because the complaint does not specify the citizenship of LLC member(s). See Pramco, LLC ex rel. CFSC Consortium, LLC v. San Juan Bay Marina, Inc., 435 F.3d 51, 54 (1st Cir. 2006); see also Americold Realty Trust v. Conagra Foods, Inc., 136 S. Ct. 1012, 1015 (2016). Within fourteen (14) days of this Order, the plaintiff shall submit a filing specifying the citizenship of LLC member(s) sufficient to establish the basis for diversity jurisdiction. (Halley, Taylor) (Entered: 11/23/2016)

Judge George A. O'Toole, Jr: ELECTRONIC ORDER entered. The filing contemplated by the Court's November 23, 2016 Electronic Order is overdue. The plaintiff's jurisdictional allegations in its complaint describe only the principal place of business of the LLC parties. As the Court previously explained, the allegations are insufficient for the Court to determine whether it can properly exercise jurisdiction over the matter because the citizenship of an LLC is based upon the citizenship of all of its members, not on its principal place of business. See Pramco, LLC ex rel. CFSC Consortium, LLC v. San Juan Bay Marina, Inc., 435 F.3d 51, 54 (1st Cir. 2006); see also Americold Realty Trust v. Conagra Foods, Inc., 136 S. Ct. 1012, 1015 (2016). The plaintiff shall file forthwith a submission specifying the citizenship of LLC member(s) sufficient to establish a basis for this Court to exercise diversity jurisdiction in order to avoid a dismissal for lack of subject matter jurisdiction. (Halley, Taylor) (Entered: 01/13/2017)

Judge George A. O'Toole, Jr: ELECTRONIC ORDER entered. The plaintiff has failed to respond to the Court's two orders requiring him to submit a filing specifying the citizenship of LLC member(s) sufficient to establish a basis for this Court to exercise diversity jurisdiction. (Nov. 23, 2016 Order (dkt. no. 12); Jan. 13, 2017 Order (dkt. no. 13).) Consequently, this action will be dismissed for lack of jurisdiction unless the plaintiff submits the contemplated filing, or otherwise shows cause why this action should not be dismissed, within fourteen (14) days of this Order. The Clerk shall mail a copy of this Order, along with the November 23, 2016 and January 13, 2017 Orders, directly to counsel at the address registered on CM/ECF. (Halley, Taylor) (Entered: 02/08/2017)

Judge George A. O'Toole, Jr: ELECTRONIC ORDER entered. The plaintiff has moved for default judgment as to defendant 390 Turnpike LLC pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 55 17 . The Court's Standing Order Regarding Motions for Default Judgment 10 requires that a motion for default judgment directed at fewer than all defendants be accompanied by a motion pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 54(b). Rule 54(b) provides that final judgment may be entered "as to one or more, but fewer than all, claims or parties only if the court expressly determines that there is no just reason for delay." Without any submission by the plaintiff addressing the Rule 54(b) standard, and without knowledge of the status of the bankruptcy proceedings as to defendant Peter A. Hern, the Court cannot assess whether there is "no just reason for delay." Accordingly, the plaintiff shall file a supplemental memorandum addressing whether and why the Rule 54(b) standard is met.(Halley, Taylor) (Entered: 06/29/2017)

District Judge Leo T. Sorokin: ELECTRONIC ORDER entered.The 18 Motion to Dismiss is ALLOWED INSOFAR as the Complaint contains (1) a prayer for injunctive relief and (2) seeks relief on behalf of persons other than the named Plaintiffs. The Court renders the first portion of its ruling in light of the presumption that the defendant, a public official, will act in good faith and comply with governing law as declared by the Court. See, e.g., Linan-Faye Constr. Co., Inc. v. Housing Auth. of City of Camden, 49 F.3d 915, 924 (3d Cir. 1995). The Motion is OTHERWISE DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE to the Court's consideration of the issues raised after filing of the administrative record and the submission of supplemental briefs on the schedule proposed by the parties in Doc. No. 34. The parties are excused from filing a Rule 56.1 statement. To the extent the supplemental briefs incorporate the memoranda from the Motion to Dismiss, the supplemental briefs shall cite the docket number and pages incorporated. (Montes, Mariliz) (Entered: 09/14/2017)

Electronic Clerk's Notes for proceedings held before Judge F. Dennis Saylor, IV: Status Conference held on 4/25/2017. Case called. (1) The Court heard updates on the current total of federal and state filings. (2) The Court heard updates on coordination with California state court cases. (3) The Court heard updates on issues concerning the production of plaintiff fact sheets. Regarding defendant's Motion to Compel Compliance with MDL Order No. 11 (Docket No. 627), orders to show cause will be issued to the three plaintiffs who have yet to cure their deficiencies (plaintiffs Belanger, Cleghorn, and Marotz). Defendant requested an award of attorney fees as to the plaintiffs subject to its motion to compel compliance. The Court took that request under advisement. As to defendant's Motion for Orders to Show Cause (Docket No. 621), the plaintiffs subject to that motion have either voluntarily dismissed their cases or have cured their deficiencies. The Court denied that motion as moot. Plaintiffs reported on their recently filed Motion to Enter an Order Regarding the Protocol for Resolving Plaintiffs' Discovery Deficiencies. Defendant intends to oppose that motion, and the parties expect that issue to be ripe by the May status conference. (4) The Court heard updates on depositions and the parties' attempts to reach agreement on a deposition protocol. The parties hope to present a joint deposition protocol at the May status conference. (5) The Court heard updates on discovery. Defendant reported that it anticipates filing a motion soon concerning the effect of the Courts recent ruling on the Motion to Dismiss Fraud Based Claims on the scope of discovery. (6) The Court referred plaintiff's Motion to Compel Wave 3 Documents (Docket No. 653) to Magistrate Judge Dein. That referral will be stayed for ten days to give the parties time to meet and confer and narrow the issues to be presented to the Magistrate Judge. (7) The Court heard updates on plaintiffs' motion for an Order Establishing a Common Benefit Fund. Plaintiffs have provided copies of the proposed order to all interested individual plaintiffs' counsels, and none have objected. The Court will grant the motion and issue the proposed order. (8) Defendant reported on its forthcoming proposed schedule for expert discovery and briefing. The parties will meet and confer before submitting any motion or proposed schedule to the Court. TEXT MINUTE ENTRY for proceedings held on February 20, 2020, with counsel for the parties in chambers before Magistrate Judge Michael D. Nelson. Discussion and arguments held regarding Plaintiff's request for an independent computer consultant to forensically image the defendants' electronic devices and servers. The Court indicated it would grant Plaintiff's request and directed the parties to meet and confer to reach an agreement within 30-days on the experts to conduct and supervise the protocol, at Plaintiff's cost; the devices and servers to be imaged; and the protocol for the inspection and copying of the forensic imaging of the agreed upon devices and servers, including the search terms and parameters to be utilized. The parties shall contact the undersigned magistrate judge for assistance if needed to resolve specific disputes in selecting the independent consultant and developing the protocol. In the event the defendants cannot agree to any forensic imagining protocol, Plaintiff is granted leave to file a renewed motion to compel forensic electronic discovery, without the need to re-file its previously filed brief and evidence. The parties are to notify the Court before April 17, 2020, concerning any joint request to extend case progression deadlines, if necessary. (SMN) (Entered: 02/21/2020)

MINUTE ORDER IN CHAMBERS of the Honorable Richard F. Boulware, II, on 5/22/2017. On August 12, 2016, the Ninth Circuit issued its decision on appeal in Bourne Valley Court Tr v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., 832 F.3d 1154, 1159-60 (9th Cir. 2016), holding that NRS 116 violates the Due Process Clause and is facially unconstitutional. The Court of Appeals issued its mandate in the appeal on December 14, 2016, vacating and remanding the judgment to the United States District Court, District of Nevada. The parties in Bourne Valley are seeking review in the United States Supreme Court. Bourne Valley filed its petition for writ of certiorari in the United States Supreme Court on April 3, 2017. This Court has additionally certified an issue regarding NRS 116's notice requirement to the Nevada Supreme Court. 2:16-cv-02561-RFB-PAL, ECF No. 41 . As the ultimate mandate in these cases may affect issues in the instant litigation, this proceeding is hereby STAYED. IT IS FURTHER ORDERED that 14 Motion to Dismiss is denied without prejudice to renewal after the stay is lifted. (Copies have been distributed pursuant to the NEF - BEL) (Entered: 05/22/2017)

ORDER OF DISMISSAL: The Clerk of Court is directed to reassign this matter to my docket, mail a copy of this order to Plaintiff, and note service on the docket. That part of the petition, construed as a writ of habeas corpus and brought under 28 U.S.C. \xc2\xa7 2254, is denied as prematurely filed. JUDGMENT: On February 10, 2017, the jury returned a verdict finding defendants liable for trademark infringement, and, in an advisory opinion, further found that such infringement was willful. OPINION, ORDER, AND AMENDED JUDGMENT amending 34 Judgment. The Court reaffirms its finding that defendants' infringement was willful and concludes that an award of defendants' profits is warranted. However, it concludes that an enhancement of that award is unwarranted, and it hereby amends the Judgment to hold that defendants are jointly and severally liable to plaintiffs in the amount of $1,864,337.29. The injunction entered at the time of the original Judgment remains in place, and this ruling hereby lifts the stay on the execution of the Judgment. The Court also concludes that plaintiffs are entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees as well as pre- and post-judgment interest at the applicable prime rate, and it instructs plaintiffs to submit proposed calculations of both fees and interest by July 17, 2017. Any objections thereto must be submitted by July 24, 2017, after which the Court will issue a final Amended Judgment reflecting the award of attorneys' fees and interest. (Signed by Judge Jed S. Rakoff on 7/3/2017) (Attachments: # 1 Right to appeal attachment 1, # 2 Right to appeal attachment 2)(mro) (Main Document 117 replaced on 7/10/2017) (mro). (Entered: 07/05/2017)

OPINION of USCA (Certified Copy) as to 126 Amended Notice of Appeal, filed by New York & Company Stores, Inc., New York & Company, Inc., 124 Notice of Cross Appeal, filed by 4 Pillar Dynasty LLC, Reflex Performance Resources Inc., 123 Notice of Appeal, filed by New York & Company Stores, Inc., New York & Company, Inc. USCA Case Number 17-2398 (L), 17-2399 (XAP). We vacate, however, the District Court's award of attorney's fees and prejudgment interest to Plaintiffs and its determination that this was an "exceptional" case under the Lanham Act. While this appeal was pending, we held that the standard for determining an "exceptional" case under the Patent Act, see Octane Fitness, LLC v. ICON Health & Fitness, Inc., 572 U.S. 545 (2014), applies also to cases brought under the Lanham Act, see Sleepy's LLC v. Select Comfort Wholesale Corp., 909 F.3d 519 (2d Cir. 2018). Because the District Court was not in a position to apply this holding when it ruled on this issue, we remand the case to the District Court to allow it to apply the Octane Fitness standard in the first instance. AFFIRMED in part and VACATED and REMANDED in part.. Catherine O'Hagan Wolfe, Clerk USCA for the Second Circuit. Certified: 08/08/2019. (nd) (Entered: 08/08/2019)

TRANSCRIPT RELEASE NOTICE: By Administrative Order 2008-35 the United States District Court for the Western District of Tennessee implemented a policy regarding remote access to electronic transcripts. Pursuant to that policy, as well as Civil Rule 5.2 and Criminal Rule 49.1, each party's attorney is required to review a transcript for information that should be redacted pursuant to the Judicial Conference's privacy policy which requires that Social Security numbers be redacted to show only the last four digits; birth dates should contain only the year of birth; individuals known to be minors should be referred to with initials; and financial account numbers should be redacted to the last four digits. Additionally, in criminal case files, home addresses should reveal only the city and state of residence.Our records indicate that the 90-day restriction on public access to an electronic transcript(s) in this matter will lapse within 10 days. If a redaction request is required but has not been filed, please do so immediately. Please feel free to contact our office for further guidance on this issue. Document #30: Release Date 10/12/2017. ** TEXT ONLY ORDER **On November 1, 2016, the court held a telephonic hearing on plaintiff's motion to compel discovery (dkt. 13 ). At the outset, the parties reported that they had resolved most of the issues raised in the motion, leaving only three disputed interrogatories and four disputed requests for admission on the table for the court to review. First, in discussion with both sides, the court reserved ruling on the disputed interrogatories, directed defendant to quantify to some degree the claimed undue burden of responding and directed plaintiff to prioritize the categories and types of information it seeks in these interrogatories. The court did not set deadlines but expects promptness from both sides. The parties then are to present any remaining disputes about these interrogatories to the court for decision. Next, the court denied plaintiff's motion to compel responses to RFAs 11, 12, 17 and 18 for reasons stated. Each side will bear its own costs on this motion. The parties had no other matters to bring to the court's attention. Signed by Magistrate Judge Stephen L. Crocker on 11/1/2016. (jls) (Entered: 11/01/2016)

IMPORTANT NOTICE: The United States District Court for the Southern District of West Virginia (WVSD) will be upgrading its current CM/ECF system to the Next Generation of CM/ECF (NextGen CM/ECF) on Monday, June 11, 2018. Complete information regarding the WVSD NextGen CM/ECF implementation can be found at h ttp://www.wvsd.uscourts.gov/nextgen-information. Currently, many attorneys within a firm may share a single PACER account. This will no longer be allowed with the new WVSD NextGen CM/ECF system because each attorney must have an individual upgraded PACER account that will be linked to their e-filing account. Shared PACER accounts cannot be used by WVSD e-filing attorneys once the WVSD NextGen CM/ECF system is implemented. Preparing for NextGen CM/ECF is a two-step process. Step one is to upgrade your PACER account, and step two is to link your upgraded PACER account to the WVSD e-filing account in the upgraded NextGen CM/ECF system. This notice only addresses the first step because the second step can't be completed until on or after June 11, 2018. The first step is to check and see if your PACER account is an "Upgraded" PACER account. Many PACER accounts have already been upgraded. If any of the following is true, you have an upgraded PACER account and no action is required until after the WVSD NextGen CM/ECF upgrade on June 11, 2018: 1) You have an upgraded PACER account for another NextGen court; or, 2) Your PACER account was created after August 10, 2014. If none of these are true, you must upgrade your legacy PACER account before you will be able to link your PACER account to your current WVSD CM/ECF account for your new NextGen CM/ECF account. Additional notices will be sent at a later date on how to handle the second step in this process. If you still have questions please contact the PACER Service Center at 800-676-6856 or the WVSD Clerk's Office CM/ECF Help Desk at (304)-347-3111. (mwk) (ADI)

IMPORTANT NOTICE: The United States District Court for the Southern District of West Virginia (WVSD) will be upgrading its current CM/ECF system to the Next Generation of CM/ECF (NextGen CM/ECF) on Monday, June 11, 2018. Complete information regarding the WVSD NextGen CM/ECF implementation can be found at h ttp://www.wvsd.uscourts.gov/nextgen-information. Preparing for NextGen CM/ECF is a two-step process. Step one was to upgrade your PACER account. Each attorney should have their own individual upgraded PACER account at this time. Step two will be to link your upgraded PACER account to your WVSD e-filing account in the new NextGen CM/ECF system on or after June 11, 2018. Please log in to the WVSD CM/ECF system at http://ecf.wv sd.uscourts.gov before June 7, 2018 to make sure you know your current WVSD e-filing login and password. Do not rely on your username and password being saved in your web browser, because that method will not work with the NextGen upgrade. If you do not know your login or password or have any additional questions please call the WVSD Clerk's Office CM/ECF Help Desk at (304)-347-3111. (mwk) (ADI)

IMPORTANT NOTICE: The United States District Court for the Southern District of West Virginia (WVSD) will be upgrading its current CM/ECF system to the Next Generation of CM/ECF (NextGen CM/ECF) on Monday, June 11, 2018. The WVSD CM/ECF system will be unavailable from 12:00pm (noon) on Friday, June 8, 2018 until 6:00am on Monday, June 11, 2018. Preparing for NextGen CM/ECF is a two-step process. Step one was to upgrade your PACER account. Each attorney should have their own individual upgraded PACER account at this time. Step two will be to link your upgraded PACER account to your WVSD e-filing account in the new NextGen CM/ECF system on or after June 11, 2018. Instructions for linking your account can be found at http://www.wvsd. uscourts.gov/pdfs/NextGen/WVSD_Link_PACER_to_CM-ECF.pdf. If you have any additional questions please call the WVSD Clerk's Office CM/ECF Help Desk at (304)-347-3111. (mwk) (ADI)
